P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 
Technical Assistance 
•• 
Administration 
(916) 322-5662 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
September 11, 1981 
Martha D. Kramer 
General Telephone Company of California 
P.o. Box 889 
Santa Monica, CA 
90406 
Dear Ms. Kramer: 
CommissioIl, 
Enforcement 
•• Statements of Economic Interest 
322~1 
322~ 
Thank/you for your letter of August 13, 1981, in which you indicated 
that General Telephone Company of California (GTE) will hold a luncheon 
on company facilities so that employees may "get acquainted n with 
legislators. 
You asked about the reporting requirments of GTE as a 
lobbyist employer for this event. 
2 California Administrative Code Section 18623(a} states: 
"As used in this section, "honorarium" means a payment for speak-
ing at any event, participating in a panel or seminar or engaging 
in any similar activity. 
For purposes of this section, free 
admission, food beverages and similar nominal benefits provided 
to a filer at an event at which he or she speaks, participates in 
a panel or seminar or performs a similar service, and a 
reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate travel and for 
necessary accomodations provided directly in connection with the 
event are neither payments nor exchanges." 
Unless the legislators are functioning at the luncheon as described 
above, all payments by GTE in connection with the luncheon are payments 
for or in connection with direct communication with legislative 
officials within the meaning of Government Code Section 82045(c) and 
must be reported as payments to influence legislative or administrative 
action. 
If I can be of any further assistance, please feel free to contact me. 
Yours Very Truly, 
Lynn Montgomery 
Political Reform Consultant 


, .. 
-
... 
August 13, 1981 
Ms. Lynn Montgomery 
Political Reform Consultant 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Ms. Montgomery 
c~ 
AUG J 7 Ii 26 AM \ B I 
1500A 
As you suggested in our conversation yesterday, I am submitting the question 
on reporting we discussed to you for a written reply. 
General Telephone Company of California regularly invites local legislators 
(during non-election years) to company facilities to have lunch and (~~~~ 
with 40 to 50 employees. 
These luncheons are simply to allow the leg~tor 
and constituents to "get acquainted" and do not focus on specific bill(s). 
None of the attendees are registered lobbyists. 
Our question is: Are we 
required to report the cost of these luncheons as a lobbying expense? If 
so, must we report the cost of the entire luncheon or just report costs 
allocable to the legislator and his or her staff? 
We would appreciate your response as soon as possible because some of these 
luncheons have already been held, and we do not want to be delinquent in 
our reports. 
Thank you for your help. 
Sincerely 
MARTHA D. KRAMER 
Governmental Affairs Analyst

sections.question: General Telephone Company of California regularly invites local legislators (during non-election years) to company facilities to have lunch and visit with 40 to 50 employees. These luncheons are simply to allow the legislator and constituents to "get acquainted" and do not focus on specific bill(s). None of the attendees are registered lobbyists. Our question is: Are we required to report the cost of these luncheons as a lobbying expense? If so, must we report the cost of the entire luncheon or just report costs allocable to the legislator and his or her staff?
sections.conclusion: Unless the legislators are functioning at the luncheon as described above, all payments by GTE in connection with the luncheon are payments for or in connection with direct communication with legislative officials within the meaning of Government Code Section 82045(c) and must be reported as payments to influence legislative or administrative action.
sections.question_synthetic: Must General Telephone Company of California report the cost of employee luncheons with local legislators as lobbying expenses, and if so, should they report the entire luncheon cost or only costs allocable to the legislator and staff?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: All payments by GTE in connection with the luncheon must be reported as payments to influence legislative or administrative action under Government Code Section 82045(c), unless the legislators are speaking, participating in a panel, or performing similar services as defined in California Administrative Code Section 18623(a).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated August 13, 1981) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 11, 1981). The response cites relevant regulatory authority and provides conditional guidance based on the nature of legislator participation at the event.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: General Telephone Company of California regularly invites local legislators (during non-election years) to company facilities to have lunch and visit with 40 to 50 employees. These luncheons are simply to allow the legislator and constituents to "get acquainted" and do not focus on specific bill(s). None of the attendees are registered lobbyists. Our question is: Are we required to report the cost of these luncheons as a lobbying expense? If so, must we report the cost of the entire luncheon or just report costs allocable to the legislator and his or her staff?

CONCLUSION: Unless the legislators are functioning at the luncheon as described above, all payments by GTE in connection with the luncheon are payments for or in connection with direct communication with legislative officials within the meaning of Government Code Section 82045(c) and must be reported as payments to influence legislative or administrative action.

You asked about the reporting requirments of GTE as a lobbyist employer for this event. 2 California Administrative Code Section 18623(a} states: "As used in this section, "honorarium" means a payment for speak- ing at any event, participating in a panel or seminar or engaging in any similar activity. For purposes of this section, free admission, food beverages and similar nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he or she speaks, participates in a panel or seminar or performs a similar service, and a reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accomodations provided directly in connection with the event are neither payments nor exchanges." Unless the legislators are functioning at the luncheon as described above, all payments by GTE in connection with the luncheon are payments for or in connection with direct communication with legislative officials within the meaning of Government Code Section 82045(c) and must be reported as payments to influence legislative or administrative action. August 13, 1981 Ms. Lynn Montgomery Political Reform Consultant Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Ms. Montgomery c~ AUG J 7 Ii 26 AM \ B I 1500A As you suggested in our conversation yesterday, I am submitting the question on reporting we discussed to you for a written reply. General Telephone Company of California regularly invites local legislators (during non-election years) to company facilities to have lunch and (~~~~ with 40 to 50 employees. These luncheons are simply to allow the leg~tor and constituents to "get acquainted" and do not focus on specific bill(s). None of the attendees are registered lobbyists. Our question is: Are we required to report the cost of these luncheons as a lobbying expense? If so, must we report the cost of the entire luncheon or just report costs allocable to the legislator and his or her staff? embedding.summary: The FPPC advises General Telephone Company of California that luncheon costs for meetings with local legislators must be reported as lobbying expenses unless the legislators are engaged in covered activities like speaking or panel participation. The company sought clarification on reporting requirements for employee luncheons designed for legislators and constituents to become acquainted.
